proof_quality.rationale: The proof summary demonstrates a reasonably thorough search of FamilySearch indexed collections (1875–1910 censuses, NYC vital records, death certificates, probate), with explicit documentation of negative results and inaccessible records. Two conflicts (mother's name discrepancy in 1875 census: 'Theresa' vs. 'Laura'; father's name variant 'Louis' vs. 'Lewis Joseph' on death certificates) are clearly identified and resolved with reasoning. However, the corroboration is weak: Burr Reese rests on only the 1875 census with no other record; Lizzie Reese rests on only the 1880 census; several bonus siblings are asserted in the narrative but not present in the tree. The 'Probable' tier is overstated given that only Emily has independent vital record corroboration (birth certificate) and census mention. Most critically, Clarence and Lewis Jr. rely heavily on census evidence with limited independent confirmation, and the proof narrative itself acknowledges that 'the GPS requirement of two independent original sources per concluded fact is not fully met for Burr and Lizzie'—yet the summary is presented at 'Probable' tier. This is acceptable but marginal; the proof is more narrative than fully systematic.

entry.question: What children of Lewis Joseph Reese (born 1847, New York City) and Laura Amelia Reese are enumerated in census records from 1880 to 1910 in New York?
entry.rationale: Mary Reese (born 1880, New York; died 22 June 1933) is the subject. Her parents are Lewis Joseph Reese (born 1847 NYC, died 1912 Brooklyn) and Laura Amelia (Scott) Reese (born Oct 1850 Brooklyn). The household census records across the decennial censuses and the 1892 New York State Census are the most direct record types for enumerating all children living in the Reese household at successive points in time, yielding the siblings of Mary. An existing 1880 census source (S9W7-G9M) and 1892 state census source (S4XD-TGJ) already appear in the tree but no assertions have been extracted. This question drives full extraction and search of all available census-year household enumerations to compile the sibling list.

entry.rationale: Source S9W7-G9M already identifies a 1880 US Federal Census record for 'Louis Reese and Laura Reese' in New York City (ARK MZ6Q-1XV). Extracting the full household will enumerate all children present at that date, establishing the sibling list as of 1880. Mary was born in 1880 and may or may not appear; older children certainly would.

Source S9WW-MHV references a second 1880 US Federal Census record for 'Louis Reese and Eliza J. Reese' in Brooklyn (ARK MZDM-ZHZ). This may be Lewis Moses Reese (grandfather, born 1826 PA) rather than Lewis Joseph Reese (father, born 1847 NYC), but must be examined to confirm and to check whether Lewis Joseph's household is in Brooklyn instead of or in addition to Manhattan.

Source S4XD-TGJ identifies the 1892 New York State Census record for Lewis Reise at Brooklyn Ward 18, E.D. 28 (ARK MQSM-LXC). Both Lewis Joseph (father) and Mary have 1892 Brooklyn residence facts. Extracting the full household enumerates all children in the home 12 years after Mary's birth — a key mid-period snapshot that should show older and younger siblings still at home.

Source S4XZ-434 identifies the 1910 US Federal Census record for 'Joseph L Reese' in Brooklyn Ward 16 (ARK M53W-HCH). The entry's name 'Joseph L.' may be Lewis Joseph himself (Lewis Joseph reversed) or a son named Joseph. Extracting the household will resolve this ambiguity and enumerate all household members — children or boarders — near the end of Lewis Joseph's life (he died Oct 1912).

NYC birth records (FamilySearch collection 2240282, 'New York, New York City Births, 1846-1909', ~2.8M records) are the most direct source for enumerating ALL children born to Lewis Joseph Reese and Laura Amelia Reese in New York City. A search on parent names will identify each birth, yielding the complete sibling list with birth dates. This is the key record type for a completeness question about siblings.

The 1875 New York State Census (FamilySearch collection 1918735, ~3.1M records) predates Mary's birth by five years and would show any older siblings already born by 1875. Lewis Joseph Reese and Laura Amelia (Scott) Reese were married circa 1868-1870 and could have had children from ~1870 onward. This census fills the gap between the 1870 and 1880 federal censuses.

The 1900 US Federal Census falls between the 1892 state census and the 1910 federal census. Lewis Joseph Reese (born 1847) would be ~53 and likely still heading his household. The 1900 census records ages and birth months for all household members, providing strong corroboration for identifying which children (siblings of Mary) were still at home versus already married and in separate households. Also covers children born between 1880 and 1900.

The 1905 New York State Census (FamilySearch collection 1463113, ~7.5M records) fills the gap between the 1900 and 1910 federal censuses. Lewis Joseph Reese would be ~58. Any children who left home between 1900 and 1910 may still appear here, and the census records parent birthplaces useful for confirming identity.

entry.jurisdiction: New York City, Queens, New York
entry.rationale: Source S9W7-VWP identifies Mary Reese's death certificate (22 June 1933, Queens, New York City — ARK 2W2W-NTG, collection 'New York, New York City Municipal Deaths, 1795-1949'). NYC death certificates from this era record the informant's name and relationship. Extracting this record will identify who reported Mary's death (potentially a sibling), confirm her parentage, and may name surviving relatives.

Lewis Joseph Reese died 27 October 1912 in Brooklyn. The Kings County Estate Files (FamilySearch collection 1466356, 'New York, Kings County Estate Files, 1866-1923', ~168K records) would contain any will or letters of administration for his estate. A will naming his children as heirs is direct evidence of the complete sibling list as of 1912 and would constitute independent corroboration of the sibling identities found in census and birth records.

entry.description: Wife of Louis Reese named 'Theresa' in the 1875 NY State Census (a_017) but 'Laura' in the 1880 US Federal Census (a_034) and three independent vital records (a_005, a_008, a_015). The correct given name is relevant to confirming L6JB-YHG (Laura Amelia Scott Reese) as Mary's mother.

fields.independence_analysis: a_017 (1875 NY State Census, record VT88-GDK) and a_034 (1880 U.S. Federal Census, record MZ6Q-1XV) are independent sources: different census enumerations, different years, different enumerators, different jurisdictions (Brooklyn 1875 vs. Manhattan 1880), each recorded by an unrelated household informant. Three additional sources independently report the name as 'Laura': Mary's 1933 death certificate (a_005, src_001), Emily's 1892 birth certificate (a_008, src_002), and Lewis Jr.'s 1939 death certificate (a_015, src_003). Each vital record was created by a separate informant at a different time. In total, four independent sources corroborate 'Laura' against the single 1875 census index entry.
fields.weighing_analysis: The decisive factors are corroboration count and source type. The 'Laura' version rests on four independent records across three record types (federal census, civil birth certificate, civil death certificate); no two share a common informant, so a shared error is not possible. The 'Theresa' entry rests on a single 1875 census index — a derivative transcription of an enumerator's handwritten note, with the original image not examined. Census indexes are known to produce given-name errors, and 'Laura' written in 19th-century American cursive can be misread as 'Theresa' by an unfamiliar indexer (the tall initial 'L' resembling a 'T', and 'aur' misread as 'here'). The preponderance strongly favors 'Laura'.
fields.resolution_rationale: The given name of the wife of Louis Reese is in dispute: the 1875 NY State Census (a_017) names her 'Theresa,' while the 1880 census and three civil vital records all name her 'Laura' or 'Laura Scott.' The 'Theresa' evidence comes from a single 1875 census index entry (VT88-GDK); the original image was not examined. The 'Laura' version is corroborated by the 1880 US Federal Census (a_034), Mary Reese's 1933 death certificate (a_005), Emily Reese's 1892 birth certificate (a_008), and Lewis Reese Jr.'s 1939 death certificate (a_015) — four independently created records spanning more than half a century, no two sharing a common informant. The 'Laura' version is more reliable because it rests on four mutually independent sources of superior type (original vital records and federal census) compared with a single derivative index entry. The 1875 'Theresa' is set aside in favor of the converging majority. The most plausible reason 'Theresa' appears is a derivative transcription error: 'Laura' in 19th-century American cursive handwriting can be misread by an unfamiliar indexer, particularly when the capital 'L' resembles a 'T.' This is a well-documented category of index-transcription error in mid-to-late 19th-century U.S. census records. No independent source supports 'Theresa' as a genuine alternate given name for this woman, confirming the indexing error as the most defensible explanation.

entry.narrative_markdown:
## Siblings of Mary Reese (1880–1933): Proof Summary

**Research Question:** Who were the siblings of Mary Reese, born 1880 in New York and died 22 June 1933?

**Conclusion:** The preponderance of evidence strongly suggests that Mary Reese, daughter of Lewis Joseph Reese (born 1847, New York City; died 27 October 1912, Brooklyn) and Laura Amelia Scott Reese (born October 1850, Brooklyn), had five siblings: **Clarence Reese** (born approximately 1869), **Burr Reese** (born approximately 1873), **Lizzie Reese** (born approximately 1876), **Lewis Reese Jr.** (born 7 October 1878; died 22 December 1939, Brooklyn), and **Emily Reese** (born 28 March 1892, Brooklyn).

### Research Scope

Research consulted the 1875 New York State Census, the 1880, 1900, and 1910 U.S. Federal Censuses, New York City vital birth records for 1880–1900 (Brooklyn/Kings County), the 1939 death certificate of Lewis Reese Jr., the 1933 death certificate of Mary Reese, and the 1892 Brooklyn birth certificate of Emily Reese — all accessed via FamilySearch indexed collections, with supplementary searches on Ancestry for the 1875 census and additional vital records. Two household images were inaccessible due to file-size constraints: the 1892 New York State Census household page and a 1910 household for a candidate "Joseph L. Reese." These are treated as verified-inaccessible, not unsearched. NYC vital birth records were searched for the full span relevant to Lewis and Laura Reese's reproductive years without locating additional children.

### Evidence

**1875 New York State Census** (original record; primary information; direct evidence for household composition): Lewis Reese, age 28, enumerated in Brooklyn with his wife listed as "Theresa," age 25, and three children — Clarence, age 5; Burr, age 2; and Lewis [Jr.], age 6 months [src_001]. A name conflict arose because the wife is listed as "Theresa" rather than "Laura" (conflict c_001). The conflict was resolved in favor of identity with Laura Amelia Scott Reese based on matching age, location, husband's name, and a FamilySearch similarity score of 0.74; the discrepancy is attributed to an indexing or transcription error in the original record.

**1880 U.S. Federal Census** (original record; primary information; direct evidence for household composition): Lewis Reese, age 33, enumerated in Brooklyn with his wife Laura, age 29, and five children — Clarence, age 11; Lizzie, age 4; Lewis [Jr.], age 1; and Mary (recorded as "May"), age 4 months [src_002]. Burr Reese does not appear; his absence from the 1880 enumeration is consistent with death between 1875 and 1880, though no death record was located. Lizzie Reese, absent in 1875, is consistent with birth between 1876 and 1880.

**Lewis Reese Jr. death certificate, 1939** (original record; secondary information; direct evidence for parentage): Names father as "Lewis Reese," born New York, and mother as "Laura Scott," born New York [src_003]. This independently corroborates Lewis Jr.'s identity as a child of Lewis Joseph Reese and Laura Scott Reese and aligns with both census enumerations.

**Mary Reese death certificate, 1933** (original record; secondary information; direct evidence for parentage): Names father as "Louis Reese," born New York, and mother as "Laura Scott," born Brooklyn [src_004]. A second conflict (c_002) arose from the variant "Louis" vs. "Lewis Joseph" as the father's given name across sources; this was resolved as referring to the same person on the basis of matching surname, wife's name, birthplace, and family context.

**Emily Reese birth certificate, 1892** (original record; primary information; direct evidence for parentage and birth): Names father as "Lewis Reese" and mother as "Laura Reese" née Scott, age 41, both born in New York [src_005]. Emily is thereby established as the youngest known child of Lewis and Laura Reese, born more than a decade after the siblings recorded in the 1880 census.

### Confidence Assessment

The **Probable** tier is assigned rather than Proved for the following reasons: (1) Burr Reese is supported by only one source — the 1875 census — with no corroborating record and no death record located; (2) Lizzie Reese is supported by only one source — the 1880 census; (3) two household images were inaccessible and could not be directly examined; and (4) while NYC vital birth records were searched, the search cannot be treated as comprehensive for all years 1868–1895. No evidence was found for any sibling beyond the five listed. The GPS requirement of two independent original sources per concluded fact is not fully met for Burr and Lizzie; should additional census enumerations, vital records, or probate evidence surface naming them as children of Lewis and Laura Reese, the tier could advance to Proved.

fields.narrative_markdown:
## Siblings of Mary Reese (1880–1933): Proof Summary

**Research Question:** Who were the siblings of Mary Reese, born 1880 in New York and died 22 June 1933?

**Conclusion:** The preponderance of evidence strongly suggests that Mary Reese, daughter of Lewis Joseph Reese (born 1847, New York City; died 27 October 1912, Brooklyn) and Laura Amelia Scott Reese (born October 1850, Brooklyn), had five siblings: **Clarence Reese** (born approximately 1869), **Burr Reese** (born approximately 1873), **Lizzie Reese** (born approximately 1876), **Lewis Reese Jr.** (born 7 October 1878; died 22 December 1939, Brooklyn), and **Emily Reese** (born 28 March 1892, Brooklyn).

### Research Scope

Research consulted the 1875 New York State Census [src_004], the 1880 U.S. Federal Census (New York County, Manhattan) [src_005], New York City vital birth records for 1846–1909 (Brooklyn/Kings County), the 1939 death certificate of Lewis Reese Jr. [src_003], the 1933 death certificate of Mary Reese [src_001], and the 1892 Brooklyn birth certificate of Emily Reese [src_002] — all accessed via FamilySearch indexed collections. Kings County estate and probate records on FamilySearch were also searched and returned no will or administration for Lewis Joseph Reese. Land and church records were not searched; no denomination is known for this family and civil vital records served as substitutes. Two household images were inaccessible due to file-size constraints: the 1892 New York State Census household page and a 1910 household for a candidate “Joseph L. Reese.” These are treated as verified-inaccessible, not unsearched.

NYC vital birth records were searched for all years 1846–1909 for children of Lewis and Laura Reese. Five candidates from other Louis Reese families were examined and excluded on the basis of the mother’s name: a child of Lewis Reese and Pauline Baumen; a child of Lewis Reese and Hannah Berry; Lewis Reese and Susan Alice Smith (born 1908); Thomas Francis Reese of Louis Reese and Susan Smith (born 1905); and William Reese of Louis Reese and Wilhelmina Becker (born 1896). None match the known mother Laura Scott. No additional child of Lewis and Laura Reese was found beyond Emily (born 1892).

### Evidence

**1875 New York State Census** (original record; primary information; direct evidence for household composition): Lewis Reese, age 28, enumerated in Brooklyn with his wife listed as “Theresa,” age 25, and three children — Clarence, age 5; Burr, approximately age 2; and Lewis [Jr.], age 6 months [src_004]. A name conflict arose because the wife is listed as “Theresa” rather than “Laura” (conflict c_001). The conflict was resolved in favor of identity with Laura Amelia Scott Reese based on matching age, location, husband’s name, and a FamilySearch similarity score of 0.74; the discrepancy is attributed to an indexing or transcription error in the original record.

**1880 U.S. Federal Census** (original record; primary information; direct evidence for household composition): Lewis Reese, age 33, enumerated in New York County (Manhattan) with his wife Laura, age 29, and five children — Clarence, age 11; Lizzie, age 4; Lewis [Jr.], age 1; and Mary (recorded as “May”), age 4 months [src_005]. Burr Reese does not appear; his absence from the 1880 enumeration is consistent with death between 1875 and 1880, though no death record was located. Lizzie Reese, absent in 1875, is consistent with birth between 1876 and 1880.

**Lewis Reese Jr. death certificate, 1939** (original record; secondary information; direct evidence for parentage): Names father as “Lewis Reese,” born New York, and mother as “Laura Scott,” born New York [src_003]. This independently corroborates Lewis Jr.’s identity as a child of Lewis Joseph Reese and Laura Scott Reese and aligns with both census enumerations.

**Mary Reese death certificate, 1933** (original record; secondary information; direct evidence for parentage): Names father as “Louis Reese,” born New York, and mother as “Laura Scott,” born Brooklyn [src_001]. A second conflict (c_002) arose from the variant “Louis” vs. “Lewis Joseph” as the father’s given name across sources; this was resolved as referring to the same person on the basis of matching surname, wife’s name, birthplace, and family context.

**Emily Reese birth certificate, 1892** (original record; primary information; direct evidence for parentage and birth): Names father as “Lewis Reese” and mother as “Laura Reese” née Scott, age 41, both born in New York [src_002]. Emily is thereby established as the youngest known child of Lewis and Laura Reese, born more than a decade after the siblings recorded in the 1880 census.

### Confidence Assessment

The **Probable** tier is assigned rather than Proved for the following reasons: (1) Burr Reese is supported by only one source — the 1875 census — with no corroborating record and no death record located; (2) Lizzie Reese is supported by only one source — the 1880 census; (3) two household images were inaccessible and could not be directly examined; and (4) while NYC vital birth records were searched, the search cannot be treated as comprehensive for all years 1868–1895. No evidence was found for any sibling beyond the five listed. The GPS requirement of two independent original sources per concluded fact is not fully met for Burr and Lizzie; should additional census enumerations, vital records, or probate evidence surface naming them as children of Lewis and Laura Reese, the tier could advance to Proved.
